title: Monitored chaotic scattering
authors: C. W. J. Beenakker, J. Sánchez Fernán, J. Tworzydło
abstract: We extend the random-matrix theory of chaotic scattering to quantum dots whose dynamics is monitored by time-resolved measurements. Starting from a scattering matrix drawn from a circular ensemble, we construct the corresponding ensemble of Kraus operators for the monitored evolution of the many-body density matrix. In the single-particle sector the sum over measurement outcomes can be carried out algebraically, giving a discrete-time quantum master equation for the transferred charge. We solve this equation numerically and compare the resulting charge-transfer statistics with closed-form random-matrix predictions. The latter rely on an equipartition rule for monitored particles, which we formulate as a conjecture and test against the master equation.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to study quantum transport in chaotic scattering systems when the system is continuously monitored by an external measurement. It derives a quantum master equation governing the evolution and uses Random Matrix Theory to predict the statistics of the transferred charge. The key finding is that monitoring breaks transport reciprocity, providing new diagnostics for open quantum systems.
main_problem: The paper extends random-matrix theory to analyze quantum transport through chaotic scattering systems when the dynamics are subjected to continuous, time-resolved monitoring.
main_result: Monitoring breaks the reciprocity of transport measurements, and the resulting charge transfer statistics can be predicted by comparing numerical solutions of a quantum master equation against Random Matrix Theory conjectures.
method: The analysis involves deriving and solving a discrete-time quantum master equation for the density matrix, followed by applying Random Matrix Theory techniques (CUE/COE ensembles) to calculate probability distributions for transferred charge.
model_or_system: The model is a quantum dot coupled to leads, where the system's evolution is governed by a quantum channel defined by Kraus operators due to the monitoring process. The scattering is statistically modeled using the Circular Unitary Ensemble (CUE).
key_observables: Transferred charge ($T$), differential conductance ($G = dI/dV$), and the probability density functions $P(T)$ and $P(T')$.
important_parameters: Weak measurement strength ($w_m$), the ratio of lead modes ($M$ vs $N$), and the fixed parameter $\gamma = Mw^2$ in the multi-mode limit.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a weak measurement strength and relies on conjectures like the 'equipartition rule' for RMT predictions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the setup (open/closed leads), two-terminal transport configurations, and the probability density functions $P(T)$ and $P(T')$ for both single-mode and multi-mode monitoring under CUE and COE statistics.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the monitored evolution via Kraus operators to deriving the master equation. It then compares numerical solutions for charge transfer statistics ($T$) against predictions from Random Matrix Theory, culminating in generalized equipartition rules for multi-mode monitoring.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO and open quantum systems as it provides a rigorous theoretical framework for analyzing non-unitary, measurement-induced dynamics in mesoscopic conductors, extending concepts from quantum chaos to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Two-terminal transport configuration in a quantum dot. A voltage difference V = V1 −V2 between contacts 1 and 2 drives a current I through the system, corresponding to a differential conductance G = dI/dV . We seek the effect on G of a monitoring of the scattering dynamics. The monitoring breaks reciprocity, leading to a difference between the case of an asymmetric bias V1 = V , V2 = 0 and the case of a symmetric bias V1 = V/2, V2 = −V/2.

FIG. 3. Charge transfer statistics for single-mode monitoring. The two panels show the probability density functions of the transferred charge T (from mode 1 to mode 2) and T (the average of the charge transfer from mode 1 to mode 2 and from mode 2 to mode 1). The histograms are computed numerically from the master equation (5.1) for M = 100, the solid curves are derived from the CUE in the limit M →∞, assuming Eq. (5.5).
FIG. 4. Same as Fig. 3, but for unitary symmetric S drawn from the COE.

FIG. 5. Charge transfer statistics for multi-mode monitor- ing. The panels show the probability density functions of the transferred charge T and T , for CUE and COE statis- tics of the scattering matrix. The histograms are computed numerically from the master equation (4.5) for M = 100, w = 0.1 ⇒γ = Mw2 = 1, the solid curves are the RMT calculations based on Eq. (6.2).

FIG. 6. Reciprocity breaking by monitoring, quantified through the mean squared difference ∆2 β of the transferred charge from contact 1 to contact 2 and the other way around. The solid curves are the RMT results (6.4). The data points are computed numerically from the master equation (4.5) as a function of γ = Mw2, by varying M at fixed w = 0.1. (Only the range γ ≲4 was accessible numerically.)
